stories.title: Starwood Advances 226K-SF Data Center in Fairfax County, Va.
stories.excerpt: Starwood Capital Group is advancing plans for a pair of data centers in Fairfax County, Va., near the Alexandria border. The investment firm filed plans last week for the first building at its Plaza 500 development, according to the Business Journals, which cited Fairfax County records. Plaza 500 South, the proposed two-story data center, would span 226,469 square feet and rise 75 feet. The first phase of construction on the building shell is estimated to cost $5 million. An affiliate of Miami-based Starwood acquired the 34-acre site, adjacent to the Bren Mar neighborhood, for $165 million in 2022. The company is also awaiting approval for a proposed electrical substation that would sit less than 100 feet from nearby homes. The plans have drawn opposition from residents, who earlier this year proposed that Fairfax County swap the Plaza 500 property for a Chantilly site the county plans to sell to Starwood for data center development. Under that proposal, affordable housing would instead be developed near Bren Mar. County officials rejected the idea and are expected to close on the $167 million Chantilly property sale to Starwood next spring. Residents are planning to rally against the Edsall Road substation when the proposal goes before the planning commission in September. Starwood is also developing several other major data center projects in Northern Virginia. The firm is redeveloping the former Washington Commanders training facility in Herndon into two data centers near its Renaissance Tech Park, which is expected to span 2.3 million square feet at full buildout and become Fairfax County’s largest data center complex. Amazon is slated to use the property. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Starwood Capital filing 226K SF data center in Fairfax County, Va.; $165M land acquisition 2022.
Residents opposing development; proposed swap to move affordable housing to Bren Mar site instead.
Major infrastructure investment near Alexandria submarket; electrical substation planned despite neighbor proximity concerns.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group is advancing Plaza 500 South, a 226,469 SF two-story data center in Fairfax County near Alexandria, with first phase shell construction estimated at $5 million. The firm acquired the 34-acre site for $165 million in 2022 and is seeking approval for an electrical substation. Local residents have opposed the project and proposed the county swap the site for affordable housing development elsewhere.
stories.impact: Hyperscale data center development in Northern Virginia signals strong demand for tech infrastructure but competes for suburban land that could yield multifamily. Fairfax County's rejection of affordable housing swap suggests data center zoning may displace residential opportunities; developers should monitor whether similar projects reduce available land and drive multifamily supply tighter in greater D.C. suburbs.

stories.title: United Talent Agency Will Relocate NYC Offices to Empire State Building
stories.excerpt: United Talent Agency (UTA) and Empire State Realty Trust, Inc. announced that the agency will move its New York offices to the Empire State Building next summer. The new office will feature 100,948 square feet across four floors and will house the agency’s filmed entertainment, creators, news, publishing, theatre, music, speakers, sports, brand advisory and executive search businesses. “We are pleased to welcome United Talent Agency to the ‘World’s Most Famous Building’ and to support the firm’s continued growth,” said Ryan Kass, EVP, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT. “The Empire State Building fosters collaboration, attracts top talent, enhances the employee experience, and is a premier destination for the world’s most innovative and influential businesses.” Mitchell Konsker, Benjamin Bass and Harrison Potter of JLL represented UTA in the lease negotiations. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle of ESRT and Scott Klau, Erik Harris, Neil Rubin, Brent Ozarowski, Zachary Weil and Cole Gendels of Newmark represented the property owner. The post United Talent Agency Will Relocate NYC Offices to Empire State Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: United Talent Agency leasing 100,948 SF across four floors at Empire State Building, summer 2026.
Major media/entertainment tenant recommitment to Midtown office; signals tenant demand in trophy asset.
No indication of negative office fundamentals; NYC creative sector consolidating in premium addresses.
stories.summary: United Talent Agency will relocate its New York offices to the Empire State Building starting summer 2026, occupying 100,948 SF across four floors to house its filmed entertainment, creators, music, sports, and other divisions. Empire State Realty Trust and UTA highlighted the building's ability to attract top talent and foster collaboration. JLL and Newmark brokered the transaction.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to multifamily; office leasing strength in Midtown suggests the creative/media sector remains NYC-anchored, supporting sustained demand for premium rental housing near transit and employment hubs. No immediate negative signal on office vacancies that might trigger adaptive reuse into apartments in that submarket.

stories.title: Real Estate Firm Yuco, AI Company Elite Each Take 7K SF at 757 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: Two tenants have signed on for 6,960 square feet each at Midtown East’s 757 Third Avenue, Commercial Observer has learned. Yuco Management, a New York City-based real estate development and management firm, inked a deal to take over the entire 27th floor of the 26-story Class A office building owned by New York Life Real Estate Investors, according to landlord broker JLL. Yuco will relocate to 757 Third Avenue from its current offices at 200 Park Avenue. Meanwhile, Elite, an artificial intelligence-powered legal software firm, signed a lease for the entire 22nd floor of the property. The deal seems to represent Elite’s first New York City office, as it has its headquarters in El Segundo, Calif. Cushman & Wakefield’s Mark Weiss and Jenna Catalon represented Yuco in its deal, while Adam Ardise, Stephen Bellwood and Rachel Rosenfeld, also from C&W, brokered the deal for Elite. JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Clark Finney, Simon Landmann, Kristen Morgan and Lance Yasinsky represented the landlord in each lease. “Every building has a different story to tell in today’s market,” JLL’s Konsker said in a statement. “Our role is to understand where tenant demand is headed and help ownership align its asset with those evolving expectations. The recent activity at 757 Third Avenue reflects the strength of Midtown leasing fundamentals, the quality of its ownership and the value tenants place on well-executed repositioning strategies.” The lengths of the leases and the exact asking rents were unclear, but JLL said rents in the office tower are in the high $70s per square foot. Spokespeople for C&W, Yuco Management and Elite did not immediately respond to requests for comment. News of the leasing at 757 Third Avenue comes as New York Life invests in repositioning and reintroducing the property to the market after acquiring it from BGO through a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure sale in October 2025. New York Life “continues to enhance the property with planned cosmetic renovations t
bullets: Yuco Management and Elite AI each leasing 6,960 SF full-floor suites at 757 Third Avenue, Midtown East.
Yuco relocating from 200 Park Ave; Elite's first NYC office, expanding from California HQ.
Mixed occupancy signals: enterprise/legal tech tenants signing long-term leases in Class A office.
stories.summary: Yuco Management (NYC real estate developer/operator) and Elite, an AI-powered legal software firm, each signed leases for entire floors (27th and 22nd respectively) at 757 Third Avenue in Midtown East, totaling ~14K SF. Yuco relocated from 200 Park; Elite expanded its first NYC location from El Segundo, California headquarters. New York Life Real Estate Investors owns the property.
stories.impact: Modest office leasing activity in Midtown East; tech tenant expansion into NYC supports local workforce and rental demand near transit. However, no net job creation signal—merely tenant consolidation and relocation. Marginal positive for residential demand near Grand Central area but not material to supply-demand dynamics.

stories.title: Original Developer Trades Castro Valley Multifamily Duo
stories.excerpt: Berkadia arranged the sale and acquisition financing of two value-add, neighboring Castro Valley multifamily communities with a total of 135 units. They included the Cedars, an 83-unit property that sold for $21.45 million, and Creekside Terrace, a 52-unit property that sold for $14.1 million. Senior managing director Jason Parr, managing director Scott MacDonald and director John Hansen of Berkadia San Francisco arranged the sale on behalf of Felson Companies, the original developer and longtime owner of both communities. Managing director Clay Akiwenzie and director Hank Workman of Berkadia San Francisco arranged a seven-year fixed-rate Freddie Mac loan with full-term interest-only on behalf of San Francisco-based Prime Residential. “Properties held by a single owner for over 50 years are incredibly rare in the Bay Area, and the value-add potential here drew significant investor demand,” said MacDonald. “Our investment sales team partnered closely with our mortgage banking colleagues to navigate a complex environment and deliver an exceptional outcome for our client.” The post Original Developer Trades Castro Valley Multifamily Duo appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Castro Valley, Calif.: 135-unit multifamily duo sold by Felson Companies (original developer, 50+ year hold).
The Cedars (83 units) $21.45M, Creekside Terrace (52 units) $14.1M; total ~$35.55M combined.
Prime Residential acquired both with 7-year Freddie Mac fixed-rate loan, interest-only full term; rare long-hold exit.
stories.summary: Berkadia brokered the sale of two value-add Castro Valley multifamily properties held by original developer Felson Companies for 50+ years: The Cedars (83 units, $21.45M) and Creekside Terrace (52 units, $14.1M). Prime Residential (San Francisco) acquired both with a Freddie Mac seven-year fixed-rate loan, interest-only financing. The rare long-hold exit signals investor demand for stabilized California multifamily.
stories.impact: Castro Valley—East Bay suburban market—shows strong investor appetite for value-add stabilized multifamily with below-market basis. Long-hold exit suggests prior owner reluctant to redevelop; new ownership likely to extract operational upside and potentially trigger modest rents. Signals East Bay investor confidence despite affordability crisis; limited new supply pipeline implications, but signals market still pricing in long-term fundamentals.

stories.title: Luxury Menswear Brand ISAIA Takes 13K SF at Feil’s 257 Park Avenue South
stories.excerpt: ISAIA, a luxury menswear brand out of Naples, Italy, has signed a 13,000-square-foot lease renewal on the 10th floor of the Feil Organization’s 257 Park Avenue South, the property owner announced Tuesday. Feil said the brand has “recommitted” to its corporate showroom space at the property, also known as the Gramercy Park Building. The length of the lease is for 10 years. Feil did not disclose the asking rent for the space. The average asking rent for office space in the Gramercy area was $89.09 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers data. “Continuing our relationship with ISAIA speaks to both the quality of the space and the Feil Organization’s ability to deliver unique environments that meet the needs of a diverse roster of tenants,” Feil’s Andrew Wiener, who represented the landlord in-house alongside Robert Fisher, said in a statement. “Their long-term recommitment is another testament to the strength of the property, which is currently 97 percent leased.” ISAIA was represented by Alexander Chudnoff and Henry Korzec from JLL. Chudnoff and Korzec did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “Our showroom at 257 Park Avenue South is an extension of the ISAIA brand and plays an important role in how we present our collections and connect with clients,” James Shay, president of ISAIA, said in a statement. “The unique character of the building, its prime location and the support of the Feil Organization made renewing our lease the right decision.” Situated at the corner of East 21st Street, 257 Park Avenue South is a 20-story office property with tenants including financial services firm Argentic Investment Management and fintech company AngelList. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: ISAIA luxury menswear renewed 13K SF on 10th floor, 257 Park Ave South (Gramercy Park Bldg), 10-year term.
Average asking rent Gramercy area Q2 2026: $89.09/SF; tenant commitment despite office headwinds.
Corporate showroom anchor tenant renewing; no disclosed rent implies competitive market dynamics.
stories.summary: ISAIA, an Italian luxury menswear brand, renewed its corporate showroom lease at Feil Organization's 257 Park Avenue South (Gramercy Park Building) for 13,000 SF across a 10-year term on the 10th floor. Feil did not disclose asking rent; Gramercy area average asking rent stands at $89.09/SF (Q2 2026). The renewal demonstrates tenant confidence in the submarket and property quality.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance; office lease renewal in Gramercy/Flatiron submarket. No negative supply signal. Long-term tenant commitment supports property values and neighborhood vitality, indirectly supporting residential demand, but no direct impact on multifamily fundamentals or pipeline.

stories.title: Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion
stories.excerpt: Resorts World New York City on Tuesday opened the first floor of its commercial casino, adding 1,400 slot machines less than three months after the new facility became the city’s first full-fledged casino. The move, which brings the number of slot machines onsite to 3,900, is expected to significantly increase gross gaming revenue and commensurate taxes to New York State, according to Resorts World. “Since the moment we opened, our slot machines have been incredibly popular with the public, generating more than $230 million in gross gaming revenue in just 11 weeks,” said Robert DeSalvio, president of Genting Americas East. “With these 1,400 new games, we will be able to accommodate more patrons and generate even more in taxes for the state.” The gaming operation represents the first stage of a planned $5.5-billion buildout at the site. By 2030, RWNYC intends to operate a 350,000-square-foot gaming floor with 6,000 slot machines and 800 live table games, a 7,000-seat entertainment venue, 2,000 hotel rooms and more than 10 acres of public green space. The post Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Resorts World NYC opened first casino floor, added 1,400 slots (now 3,900 total); $230M gaming revenue in 11 weeks.
Planned $5.5B buildout by 2030: 350K SF gaming floor, 6,000 slots, 800 tables, 7K-seat venue, 2K hotel rooms.
Major destination amenity expected to drive traffic and visitation to Queens/Long Island City area.
stories.summary: Resorts World New York City opened its first commercial casino floor with 1,400 new slot machines, bringing the total to 3,900, generating $230 million in gross gaming revenue in just 11 weeks. The property is executing a $5.5 billion master plan through 2030, including 350K SF gaming floor, 7,000-seat entertainment venue, 2,000 hotel rooms, and 10+ acres of public space.
stories.impact: Destination resort with 2,000 hotel rooms and entertainment venue will drive sustained foot traffic and visitor spending in Queens near Long Island City; strong secondary effect on nearby multifamily demand for both service workers and affluent residents seeking proximity to entertainment/hospitality. Developer should monitor rental rate uplift and demand in adjacent submarkets (Astoria, Sunnyside, western Queens) as resort phases complete.

stories.title: Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood
stories.excerpt: Calare Properties has acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000-square-foot industrial warehouse/flex condominium in Boston’s Hyde Park section. Terms were not disclosed. The urban property sits within a 22-acre, 10-building industrial and mixed-use site with a manufacturing history dating back to the early 1900s. 128 CRE brokered the sale. The building is nearly 50% leased to a long-term tenant. The available 24,000-square-foot space provides tenants with an opportunity to secure a long-term lease in a market where quality industrial space is scarce, according to Calare. The new ownership is executing a capital improvement plan. “Quality industrial buildings of this scale are exceptionally rare in Boston, and that scarcity is exactly what drives our strategy,” said Tim Collis, SVP at Calare. “Being part of an established, well-run campus gives us both immediate stability and long-term upside as we execute our planned capital program and long-term leasing strategy.” The post Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Calare Properties acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, 42K SF industrial/flex condo in Hyde Park, Boston.
~50% leased to long-term tenant; 24K SF available space; scarce quality industrial supply in metro.
Urban industrial campus play with capital improvement plan; rarely available large-scale Boston properties.
stories.summary: Calare Properties acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000 SF industrial warehouse/flex condo in Boston's Hyde Park neighborhood within a 22-acre, 10-building mixed-use campus. The property is ~50% leased with 24K SF vacant; Calare is executing a capital improvement plan. Market scarcity of quality industrial space at this scale drove the acquisition strategy.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. However, scarcity of industrial flex space and investor focus on campus-scale holdings in Boston's urban fringe may signal tight industrial supply and potential repurposing; if industrial-to-residential conversions become viable, developers should monitor this submarket for conversion opportunities as tech/biotech tenant demand absorbs existing supply.

stories.title: Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale
stories.excerpt: The Mogharebi Group (TMG) represented JBT Property Management in the sale of Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit multifamily community located at 475-585 W. Sierra Ave. in Fresno, to a private buyer for $44 million. TMG’s team of EVP Otto Ozen, SVP Brian Nakamura and Svp Nazli Santana represented the seller. “As a first-time-on-the-market property since its construction in 1986, the asset offered a rare opportunity to acquire a well-maintained, large-scale property directly from original ownership,” said Nakamura. “With its substantial unit count in a supply‑constrained market, the property immediately enhances the buyer’s regional presence—supporting stronger operational alignment, improved management efficiency, and meaningful performance gains, all without requiring significant capital investment.” Maroa Park Apartments benefits from immediate income upside through operational efficiencies while benefiting from strong in-place market fundamentals, relative affordability, and a strategic Central Valley location that continues to support long-term population and employment growth, according to TMG The post Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Maroa Park Apartments, Fresno: 248 units, first-time market sale since 1986 construction, $44M ($177K/unit).
Original ownership held 40 years; supply-constrained market with strong long-term fundamentals.
Central Valley location supports population/employment growth; operational upside through efficiency gains.
stories.summary: TMG represented the sale of Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit well-maintained community in Fresno (475-585 W. Sierra Ave.), held by original owner JBT Property Management since 1986, to a private buyer for $44 million (~$177K per unit). The rare direct-from-developer large-scale asset in a supply-constrained market offers immediate operational upside and strong Central Valley growth fundamentals.
stories.impact: Central Valley Fresno market signals tight multifamily supply and strong investor confidence in regional employment/population growth (agricultural, logistics, tech corridor). Valuation (~$177K/unit stabilized) and buyer focus on operational efficiency suggest limited new construction and high replacement value; developers should model new supply in Fresno and surrounding Central Valley metros—strong demand signals but entitlement/construction cost challenges likely limit new unit delivery.

stories.title: Oceanwide Plaza Sale Set to Advance After L.A. Drops Objections
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles’ Oceanwide Plaza, a massive downtown development stuck in limbo so long it became tagged and transformed into the infamous Graffiti Towers, has been cleared for sale. The long process of selecting a new developer after the original Chinese developer went bankrupt may finally be coming to a close. KPC Group, a Southern California real estate firm headed by Dr. Kali Chaudhuri, and Lendlease Americas will contribute $517 million in cash and credit to acquire the unfinished project, and agreed to spend roughly $800 million to finish the trio of towers, located downtown near Crypto.com Arena. “I will be delighted to enter an order confirming the plan,” said Deborah Saltzman, a federal bankruptcy judge, during an hours-long hearing in a Downtown Los Angeles courtroom on Monday. After a break for negotiations, the parties returned to the courtroom for a brief afternoon session confirming the details of the arrangement. KPC said it will begin graffiti removal on Wednesday. The plan approval comes despite the city attorney’s objections to the KPC/Lendlease bid. In a May filing, the city, in its role as a creditor and governmental entity overseeing regulations and entitlements for the project, noted after multiple meetings, site visits and analysis that the bidder didn’t offer a complete plan that showed they had the funding and resources to fully finish. The City Attorney’s Office dropped its objections to the modified bankruptcy plan Monday after securing stronger commitments from the proposed buyer. The final version of the court’s orders will be filed Tuesday. KPC has a six-month window to complete the sale. Otherwise a backup buyer can be selected. In recent months, a competing bid by Sean Burton‘s CityView was seen as a potential backup. KPC is also required, within 90 days, to remove the graffiti that has adorned the tower since 2024, when taggers began climbing and bombing the towers. Long considered an eyesore, a potential embarrassment during the u
bullets: Oceanwide Plaza (L.A. downtown): KPC Group & Lendlease acquire unfinished $1B luxury project, $517M equity, ~$800M completion cost.
Federal bankruptcy court approval advancing; stalled since 2019 after Chinese developer funding collapse.
Three-tower near Crypto.com Arena; downtown L.A. supply recovery; long-delayed luxury delivery.
stories.summary: Los Angeles federal bankruptcy court is poised to approve the sale of Oceanwide Plaza, the long-stalled $1 billion luxury three-tower downtown development, to KPC Group and Lendlease Americas. The partners will contribute $517 million in cash/credit to acquire the unfinished project and commit ~$800 million to complete the towers near Crypto.com Arena, breaking a deadlock that began in 2019.
stories.impact: Downtown L.A. luxury residential supply finally moving off the shelf after 5+ year stall; completion of 300–400 units (estimated) will depress rents in downtown luxury segment and displace demand to adjacent neighborhoods (Arts District, Staples Center area). Developers should monitor completion timeline and pricing; potential ripple effects on Koreatown, Central City East, and Bunker Hill submarkets. Market absorption risk if delivered concurrently with other downtown projects; strong signal that downtown L.A. remains development target despite recent stagnation.

stories.title: AI Company Mercor Takes 26K SF at Durst’s One World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: One World Trade Center is almost 100 percent leased thanks to the latest deal with an artificial intelligence company, Commercial Observer has learned. Mercor, which employs human experts to train AI algorithms, has inked a five-year, 25,550-square-foot deal on the 77th floor of the Lower Manhattan office tower, the Durst Organization announced Tuesday. Durst owns the 104-story building along with the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. The exact asking rent was not disclosed, but a source close to the deal told CO that the asking rent was in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. Durst was represented in-house by Eric Engelhardt, Karen Rose and Sayo Kamara, along with David Falk, Peter Shimkin, Hal Stein, Nathan Kropp and Paige Raisides from Newmark. Harry Singer from CBRE represented Mercor. The brokers did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “One World Trade Center was built for forward-looking, cutting-edge companies looking for a world-class home in New York City,” Jody Durst, president of Durst, said in a statement. “We look forward to welcoming Mercor to the building.” Mercor’s website says its headquarters is located in San Francisco, and does not list a New York City address. However, company CEO Brendan Foody previously posted on LinkedIn that the company would be expanding to New York City in January. It is unclear whether Foody was referencing the deal at One World Trade Center. “New York puts us close to some of the most important enterprises in the world, and to some of the highest caliber talent,” Koby Twist, special initiatives lead at Mercor, said in a statement. “One World Trade Center gives our team a home at the center of both.” One World Trade Center, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, was built by the Port Authority and Durst in a public-private partnership. Other recent tenants include fashion and culture publication W Magazine, global financial services company Ameriprise Financial, and energy- and infrastruct
bullets: Mercor AI company leasing 25,550 SF on 77th floor, One World Trade Center; 5-year term, $80s–$90s/SF asking.
One WTC now ~100% leased; trophy Manhattan office securing cutting-edge tech tenant.
Lower Manhattan office/residential adjacency; strong demand for premium NYC workspace near transit.
stories.summary: Mercor, an AI company that employs human experts to train algorithms, signed a five-year, 25,550 SF lease on the 77th floor of One World Trade Center (Durst Organization). The deal brings the building to near-100% occupancy; asking rent was in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. Durst and Port Authority of New York and New Jersey own the 104-story Lower Manhattan tower.
stories.impact: Near-full occupancy at One WTC reinforces strong Manhattan office demand, especially for tech/AI tenants seeking premium downtown addresses. Indirect positive for Lower Manhattan multifamily: sustained office employment and amenity investment support residential leasing in adjacent TriBeCa, Battery Park City, and FiDi submarkets. No supply-side impact; signals healthy tenant demand but not new pipeline or construction impulses.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Ponce-Davis mansion sale: $18.9M, telecom exec buyer, 10K SF, built 2026.
Miami Wynwood commercial: $24.5M for 13K SF mixed-use, buyer developer/investor Moishe Mana.
South Florida luxury residential and commercial showing strong investor/buyer activity across segments.
stories.summary: South Florida recorded two high-value deals: a $18.9 million Ponce-Davis mansion (10K SF, 2026 build, 1-acre lot, telecom executive buyer) and a $24.5 million Wynwood mixed-use property (13K SF, buyer linked to developer Moishe Mana). Additional residential deals in Jupiter at $10.5M and $8M appreciated year-over-year, reflecting strong high-net-worth buyer demand in the region.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. Luxury residential and commercial investor activity in Miami/Wynwood signals affluent buyer confidence and ongoing gentrification in mixed-use neighborhoods; may pressure land costs for multifamily development in Wynwood and near Ponce-Davis. Mana's continued investment in Wynwood suggests mixed-use/cultural district growth that could support rental demand; monitor zoning/entitlement shifts in these submarkets.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing & Capstone Equities: 316 affordable units, Warner Center (Woodland Hills), 21155 Califa St.
100% affordable development; expedited approval under L.A. Executive Directive 1; seven-story, 300K+ SF.
Follows Elysian/Capstone office-to-residential conversion (Balboa Blvd); Stan Kroenke 52-acre mixed-use nearby.
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are developing The Green at Warner Center in Woodland Hills, a 316-unit 100% affordable housing project (seven stories, 300K+ SF) on a 2.7-acre site at 21155 Califa Street. The all-affordable model qualifies for expedited approval under L.A. Executive Directive 1. This follows a prior Elysian-Capstone office-to-residential conversion on Balboa Boulevard and aligns with Stan Kroenke's 52-acre mixed-use development anchoring the Warner Center area.
stories.impact: San Fernando Valley Warner Center becoming affordable housing hub amid major private mixed-use investment (Kroenke). Elysian/Capstone's repeated affordable conversions signal strong subsidy/tax credit availability and municipal appetite for affordable density. New 316 units will tighten Valley supply and may elevate market-rate rents by constraining replacement inventory; developers should monitor whether Kroenke's mixed-use success (retail, office, hotel) accelerates zoning/entitlement appetite for multifamily in adjacent submarkets—potential for significant new supply pipeline across Warner Center corridor.

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill, Texas (Austin suburb) selected Griffin Swinerton to lead downtown revamp; target 2028 start.
Fast-growing suburb tripled size since 2020; community-driven masterplan adopted 2024 for historic preservation.
Similar precedent: Griffin Swinerton on Downtown East, Pflugerville (29 acres); joint venture model.
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, one of the nation's fastest-growing Austin-area suburbs, selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton (JV of Griffin Structures and Swinerton) to lead a downtown transformation starting 2028. The joint venture will function as city staff, handling infrastructure, historic preservation, financing evaluation, and community engagement. Liberty Hill has tripled in size since 2020 and adopted a downtown masterplan in 2024 emphasizing walkability and gathering spaces.
stories.impact: Rapid suburban growth (Austin sprawl) creates pent-up demand for infill downtown mixed-use; Griffin Swinerton's redevelopment engagement model suggests multifamily will likely be core to Liberty Hill downtown vision. Developers should monitor 2028+ entitlement/zoning announcements; transit-oriented affordable/workforce housing likely target given Austin affordability crisis. Strong pipeline signal for Central Texas; model replicable across fast-growing Austin suburbs (Pflugerville, Leander, Round Rock).

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK Companies: 910 South Wells, Chicago South Loop, 28-story, 350 units + 3.3K SF retail; piles permit secured.
Part of truncated 8-acre Riverline master plan ($2B original vision, halved 2018 after Lendlease split).
CMK recently drew $83M construction loan (Wintrust); 1010 South Wells (388 units) under construction nearby.
stories.summary: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells Street mixed-use development (28 stories, 350 residential units, 3.3K SF retail) just secured its piles permit and is cleared for foundation work. Gensler designed the building, which includes parking, resident amenities, and riverwalk connection. The project is part of CMK's eight-acre Riverline master plan (halved from original $2B vision in 2018). CMK secured an $83 million construction loan from Wintrust for the nearby 1010 South Wells (388-unit apartment building, already under construction).
stories.impact: South Loop Chicago multifamily pipeline acceleration: 350 + 388 = 738 units from CMK alone, both under active construction/entitlement. Strong supply signal; developer should monitor absorption and rent trajectory post-delivery (2026–2027 likely). Riverline completion will add significant new density to South Loop residential market; positive for nearby retail/restaurant, but competitive pressure on Class A rents. Monitor Lendlease's northern parcel (Cooper, Reed) for concurrent delivery; combined 1,000+ unit delivery risk in tight South Loop submarket.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas: $800M wellness resort on 1500 S Riverfront Blvd, 24-acre, 500K SF; 2027 groundbreaking target.
Adding skybridge between buildings, new non-residential zones; next to Water Commons park and Harold Simmons Park.
Prior controversy: Misrepresented experience to Ontario; only one Bucharest spa built; development credibility questions.
stories.summary: Austrian wellness company Therme Dallas is pursuing city approval for design adjustments to its $800 million resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard, including a pedestrian skybridge and expanded non-residential zones. The 24-acre, 500K SF project (thermal baths, pools, saunas, restaurants, retractable glass roof) targets a 2027 groundbreaking. The company's credibility was questioned when a New York Times article revealed it had misrepresented its development track record to Ontario; Therme has only completed one facility (Bucharest), not six as claimed.
stories.impact: Therme Dallas is a destination amenity/leisure play, not multifamily-relevant per se. However, Trinity River waterfront development alongside Water Commons park will drive area perception uplift and attract regional visitation/spending. If delivered, ripple effect on nearby multifamily demand in uptown/downtown Dallas. Significant credibility/execution risk: developer should monitor permit progress and financing certainty before underwriting projects in immediate submarket. Completion delay likely; may not influence supply/demand until 2028–2029 at earliest.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development: $28M mid-construction financing for The Albritton mixed-use, Friendswood (25 mi SE Houston).
111 multifamily units, 11K SF retail, 9K SF office; completion 2028; 70% LTV Freddie Mac-style debt.
Developer also building $750M Friendswood City Center (106 acres) and $1B Magnolia Town Center; active regional pipeline.
stories.summary: Tannos Development Group secured $28 million in mid-construction financing from S3 Capital (arranged by Concord Summit Capital) for The Albritton mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas (25 miles SE of downtown Houston). The debt carries a 70% LTV and funds remainder of construction; the project includes 111 multifamily units, 11K SF retail, 9K SF office space, targeting 2028 completion. Tannos is also developing the $750 million Friendswood City Center and $1 billion Magnolia Town Center in the region.
stories.impact: Houston exurban sprawl: Friendswood (42K residents, straddling Harris/Galveston counties) benefiting from Houston metro job/population diffusion; $28M mid-construction financing signals lender confidence in completion risk and market absorption. Tannos's three concurrent mega-projects ($750M, $1B, plus The Albritton) suggest strong developer conviction on outer-loop Houston demand. Implies multifamily supply pipeline in Friendswood/Magnolia; monitor absorption and rent growth in adjacent submarkets; competitive pressure likely as projects deliver 2027–2029. Strong signal for Texas regional growth but supply concentration risk.
